verse.arabic_text: وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۖ وَلَلدَّارُ ٱلْءَاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ
verse.translation: And the worldly life is not but amusement and diversion; but the home of the Hereafter is best for those who fear Allah, so will you not reason?

theme_question.correct: The true value and permanence of the Hereafter compared to the fleeting nature of worldly life.
options: The obligation to earn a living and enjoy the lawful pleasures of the world.
The true value and permanence of the Hereafter compared to the fleeting nature of worldly life.
The prohibition of specific worldly amusements like music and games.
A command to completely abandon the world and live in seclusion.

educational_notes.verse_context: This verse is part of a larger discourse addressing the disbelievers of Makkah who were engrossed in their worldly life and rejected the Prophet's (ﷺ) message about the Hereafter. It serves as a powerful rhetorical question, urging them to use their intellect to understand the true reality of existence.
educational_notes.theme_explanation: The theme is a fundamental Quranic principle: the hierarchy of values between the dunya (this world) and the akhirah (the Hereafter). The verse uses the metaphor of 'amusement and diversion' (لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ) to describe the world's deceptive and temporary nature. True, lasting success and goodness are reserved for those who have Taqwa (God-consciousness, piety) and prioritize the life to come. The final question, 'so will you not reason?' (أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ), challenges the listener to reflect deeply on their priorities.
educational_notes.surah_overview: Surah Al-An'am ('The Cattle') is the 6th chapter of the Quran and is unique as it was revealed entirely in Makkah in one sitting. Its central theme is Tawhid (the absolute Oneness of Allah) and the refutation of shirk (polytheism). It addresses core tenets of faith, including Allah's sovereignty, creation, prophethood, and the reality of resurrection and judgment, often using the signs in the natural world as proof of the Creator's power and wisdom. The surah's name comes from the discussion of various pagan superstitions related to cattle (ayahs 136-140).
